title: Multi-Cloud Security Monitoring Strategy
content:
# Multi-Cloud Security Monitoring Strategy

## Purpose

This procedure defines a unified approach to security monitoring across multi-cloud environments spanning AWS, Azure, and GCP. Organizations operating in multiple clouds face challenges in maintaining consistent visibility, correlating events across providers, and enforcing uniform security policies. This guide addresses those challenges with practical implementation guidance.

## Scope

All cloud environments under SOC monitoring where the organization uses two or more cloud providers. This includes IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, and hybrid cloud deployments.

## Architecture Overview

### Unified Logging Architecture

A multi-cloud security monitoring strategy requires centralizing logs from all providers into a single analytics platform:

```
[AWS CloudTrail] ----\
[AWS GuardDuty]  ----\
[AWS VPC Flow Logs] --\--> [Central SIEM / Security Data Lake]
[Azure Activity Log] -/-->  (Sentinel, Splunk, Chronicle, Elastic)
[Azure AD Logs]    --/-->   |
[GCP Audit Logs]   -/-->    v
[GCP VPC Flow Logs]/    [Correlation Engine]
                              |
                              v
                     [SOC Analyst Dashboard]
                     [Automated Response]
```

### Log Collection by Provider

**AWS Log Sources:**

| Log Source | Collection Method | Latency |
|-----------|-------------------|----------|
| CloudTrail (Management Events) | S3 + EventBridge -> SIEM | 5-15 min |
| CloudTrail (Data Events) | S3 -> SIEM | 5-15 min |
| GuardDuty Findings | EventBridge -> SIEM | Near real-time |
| VPC Flow Logs | CloudWatch Logs -> SIEM | 1-5 min |
| Security Hub Findings | EventBridge -> SIEM | Near real-time |
| WAF Logs | Kinesis Firehose -> SIEM | Near real-time |
| Route 53 DNS Logs | CloudWatch Logs -> SIEM | 1-5 min |

**Azure Log Sources:**

| Log Source | Collection Method | Latency |
|-----------|-------------------|----------|
| Azure Activity Log | Diagnostic Settings -> Event Hub -> SIEM | 1-5 min |
| Azure AD Sign-in Logs | Diagnostic Settings -> Event Hub -> SIEM | 1-5 min |
| Azure AD Audit Logs | Diagnostic Settings -> Event Hub -> SIEM | 1-5 min |
| NSG Flow Logs | Storage Account -> SIEM | 5-15 min |
| Defender for Cloud Alerts | Event Hub -> SIEM | Near real-time |
| Key Vault Logs | Diagnostic Settings -> Event Hub -> SIEM | 1-5 min |

**GCP Log Sources:**

| Log Source | Collection Method | Latency |
|-----------|-------------------|----------|
| Cloud Audit Logs | Pub/Sub Sink -> SIEM | 1-5 min |
| VPC Flow Logs | Pub/Sub Sink -> SIEM | 5-15 min |
| SCC Findings | Pub/Sub Notifications -> SIEM | Near real-time |
| Cloud DNS Logs | Pub/Sub Sink -> SIEM | 1-5 min |
| Load Balancer Logs | Cloud Storage -> SIEM | 5-15 min |

## Normalized Event Schema

To correlate events across clouds, normalize events to a common schema. Use OCSF (Open Cybersecurity Schema Framework) or ECS (Elastic Common Schema) as the foundation:

### Identity Normalization

| Concept | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---------|-----|-------|-----|
| User identity | IAM User ARN | User Principal Name | User email |
| Service identity | IAM Role ARN | Managed Identity / Service Principal | Service Account email |
| Admin role | IAM admin policies | Global Administrator | Organization Admin |
| MFA status | CloudTrail MFAAuthenticated | Sign-in log MFA result | Google Workspace MFA status |
| Source IP | CloudTrail sourceIPAddress | Sign-in log ipAddress | Audit log callerIP |

### Event Type Normalization

| Normalized Event | AWS CloudTrail | Azure Activity Log | GCP Audit Log |
|-----------------|----------------|-------------------|---------------|
| User created | CreateUser | Create user | CreateServiceAccount |
| Role assigned | AttachUserPolicy | Add member to role | SetIamPolicy |
| Resource created | RunInstances, CreateBucket | Create deployment | instances.insert |
| Login success | ConsoleLogin (no error) | Sign-in success | UserLoginService |
| Login failure | ConsoleLogin (Failed) | Sign-in failure (error) | LoginFailureEvent |
| Config change | ModifyInstanceAttribute | Update resource | instances.setMetadata |
| Logging disabled | StopLogging | Delete diagnostic setting | SetSink (delete) |

## Cross-Cloud Correlation Rules

### Rule 1: Impossible Multi-Cloud Travel

Detect when the same user identity authenticates to multiple cloud providers from different geographic locations within an impossible timeframe.

```
IF user_email matches across providers
AND login_success in AWS from Location_A at Time_1
AND login_success in Azure from Location_B at Time_2
AND geographic_distance(Location_A, Location_B) > possible_travel(Time_2 - Time_1)
THEN alert: "Impossible Multi-Cloud Travel"
```

### Rule 2: Cross-Cloud Privilege Escalation

Detect when an identity escalates privileges across cloud boundaries:

```
IF IAM_change_event in Cloud_A (role assignment, policy change)
AND same_user performs privileged_action in Cloud_B within 1 hour
AND neither action is associated with a change management ticket
THEN alert: "Cross-Cloud Privilege Escalation"
```

### Rule 3: Coordinated Data Exfiltration

Detect simultaneous data access across clouds:

```
IF large_data_transfer from AWS S3 (>1GB)
AND large_data_transfer from Azure Storage (>1GB)
AND both from same source_ip or same user_identity
AND within 4 hour window
THEN alert: "Coordinated Cross-Cloud Data Transfer"
```

### Rule 4: Multi-Cloud Logging Disruption

Detect attempts to disable logging across providers:

```
IF logging_disabled_event in any cloud
AND (StopLogging OR DeleteTrail OR delete_diagnostic_setting OR delete_log_sink)
THEN alert: "Cloud Logging Disruption" (Critical if multiple clouds affected)
```

### Rule 5: Shadow Cloud Account Detection

Detect when organizational identities access cloud accounts not in the known inventory:

```
IF cloud_login_event
AND account_id NOT IN known_organization_accounts
AND user_email matches organization domain
THEN alert: "Unknown Cloud Account Access"
```

## Consistent Policy Enforcement

### Guardrail Parity Matrix

Ensure equivalent security controls exist across all cloud providers:

| Control | AWS Implementation | Azure Implementation | GCP Implementation |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Prevent public storage | S3 Block Public Access (account) | Storage account public access disabled | Organization policy: storage.publicAccessPrevention |
| Enforce encryption | SCP deny unencrypted resources | Azure Policy require encryption | Organization policy: constraints/compute.requireOsLogin |
| Require MFA | IAM policy condition aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent | Conditional Access policy | Google Workspace: Enforce MFA |
| Restrict regions | SCP deny non-approved regions | Azure Policy allowed locations | Organization policy: constraints/gcp.resourceLocations |
| Block admin key creation | SCP deny iam:CreateAccessKey for admin | Conditional Access: Block legacy auth | Organization policy: constraints/iam.disableServiceAccountKeyCreation |
| Network segmentation | VPC + Security Groups + NACLs | VNet + NSGs + Azure Firewall | VPC + Firewall Rules + VPC Service Controls |

### Configuration Drift Detection

1. Deploy CSPM tools that support all three major clouds (Prisma Cloud, Wiz, Orca, or open-source tools like Prowler + ScoutSuite)
2. Map controls to a common framework (CIS Benchmarks exist for AWS, Azure, and GCP)
3. Run daily compliance scans and alert on drift from baseline
4. Implement auto-remediation for critical misconfigurations (public storage, open admin ports)

## SOC Operations Model

### Alert Prioritization

Multi-cloud alerts should be prioritized based on:
1. **Data sensitivity**: Production data stores > staging > development
2. **Blast radius**: Organization-level changes > account-level > resource-level
3. **Cross-cloud correlation**: Events spanning multiple clouds are inherently higher priority
4. **Persistence indicators**: IAM changes, logging disruption, trust relationship modifications

### Analyst Skill Requirements

SOC analysts monitoring multi-cloud environments need:
- Proficiency in at least two major cloud IAM models
- Ability to query logs using KQL (Azure), CloudWatch Insights (AWS), and Cloud Logging (GCP)
- Understanding of cloud networking concepts across providers
- Familiarity with infrastructure-as-code patterns (Terraform, CloudFormation, ARM/Bicep)

### Runbook Mapping

Maintain per-cloud runbooks for common scenarios, with a multi-cloud correlation overlay:

| Scenario | AWS Runbook | Azure Runbook | GCP Runbook | Multi-Cloud |
|----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| Credential compromise | AWS Cred Leak SOP | Azure AD Compromise | GCP SA Key Rotation | Cross-cloud session audit |
| Data exposure | S3 Exposure Runbook | Storage Exposure | GCS Exposure | Unified notification |
| Privilege escalation | IAM Investigation | Azure RBAC Review | GCP IAM Review | Cross-cloud role mapping |

## MITRE ATT&CK Mapping

- **T1078.004** — Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts
- **T1562.008** — Impair Defenses: Disable Cloud Logs
- **T1537** — Transfer Data to Cloud Account
- **T1580** — Cloud Infrastructure Discovery
- **T1098** — Account Manipulation

- [OCSF Schema](https://schema.ocsf.io/)
- [CIS Benchmarks for AWS, Azure, GCP](https://www.cisecurity.org/benchmark)
- [MITRE ATT&CK Cloud Matrix](https://attack.mitre.org/matrices/enterprise/cloud/)
- [CSA Cloud Controls Matrix v4](https://cloudsecurityalliance.org/research/cloud-controls-matrix/)
